---
title: "Generalised linear models: the foundation"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
A **linear model** $y = X\beta + \varepsilon$ is solved by projecting $y$ onto the
columns of $X$ — i.e. by solving the **normal equations** $X^{\top}X\,\beta = X^{\top}y$.
A **generalised** linear model lets the response be counts, probabilities, or
positive values instead of Gaussian. We fit it by **IRLS**: repeatedly turn the
non-Gaussian problem into a *weighted* least-squares problem
$X^{\top} W X\,\beta = X^{\top} W z$ and re-solve, until it stops moving. The one
new idea this page adds is that IRLS is not a trick — it is **Fisher scoring**,
and the weights $W$ and working response $z$ are *forced* by the exponential
family's mean–variance identity. That single $p\times p$ solve — plus $W$ and $z$
— is the machinery the entire suite is built on.
:::

This is the bottom rung of [the ladder](index.qmd). Read it carefully: every
later model is this chapter plus one new idea. It sits directly on top of
[foundations](foundations.qmd) (the variance and the exponential family) and
hands its linear-algebra chores to [solvers](solvers.qmd) (the small dense solve,
damped Newton).

## The linear model is a projection

Suppose each observation $i$ (a subject, a scan) has a response $y_i$ and a row
of explanatory numbers $x_i = (x_{i1}, \dots, x_{ip})$ — an intercept, an age, a
group indicator, and so on. A linear model claims the response is a weighted sum
of those numbers plus noise:

$$
y_i = x_i \beta + \varepsilon_i,
\qquad\text{stacked:}\qquad
y = X\beta + \varepsilon,
$$ {#eq-linmod}

where $X$ is the $N\times p$ **design matrix** (one row per observation) and
$\beta\in\mathbb{R}^p$ are the unknown coefficients. "Fit the model" means: find
the $\beta$ making $X\beta$ as close to $y$ as possible in the least-squares
sense, $\min_\beta \lVert y - X\beta\rVert^2$.

You already know the answer from linear algebra. $X\beta$ ranges over the column
space of $X$; the closest point to $y$ in that subspace is the **orthogonal
projection** of $y$, and the residual $y - X\beta$ is orthogonal to every column
of $X$. Writing "orthogonal to every column" as $X^{\top}(y - X\beta) = 0$ gives
the **normal equations**

$$
X^{\top}X\,\beta = X^{\top}y .
$$ {#eq-normal}

So fitting a linear model *is* solving one $p\times p$ linear system. $p$ — the
number of coefficients — is usually small (a handful), even when $N$ is large.
Hold onto that: the object we actually invert is the tiny $p\times p$ matrix
$X^{\top}X$, never anything of size $N$.

## Mass-univariate: one design, a whole brain of responses

In neuroimaging we rarely have one response vector; we have one *per voxel* —
hundreds of thousands of them — all sharing the **same** design $X$ (the same
group-level model applied everywhere). Stack them as a matrix $Y$ of shape
$V\times N$ ($V$ elements, $N$ observations).

Because $X$ is shared, @eq-normal has the same matrix $X^{\top}X$ for every
element. Factor that $p\times p$ matrix **once** and every element's coefficients
are a couple of matrix multiplications away:

$$
\hat B = Y\,X\,(X^{\top}X)^{-1} .
$$

No per-element factorisation — one $p\times p$ inverse fits the whole image. This
"shared design, per-element response" shape is why `glm_fit` takes a vectorised
fast path for the ordinary-least-squares case. Reading `glm.py`, the Gaussian
identity-link branch (`_ols_fit`) really does form $X^{\top}WX$ once, invert it
once via `small_inv_logdet`, and recover all $V$ coefficient vectors as
`(Y @ Xw) @ xtwx_inv`. Every other family falls back to a per-element
(`vmap`-ed) IRLS — the subject of the rest of this page. The shape recurs at
every rung of the ladder: the shared piece is computed once; only the response
varies.

## When the response isn't Gaussian

Ordinary least squares quietly assumes the noise is Gaussian with constant
variance. That is wrong for much of the data we model:

- **binary** outcomes (present / absent) live in $\{0,1\}$;
- **counts** (number of events) are non-negative integers whose variance grows
  with their mean;
- **positive** continuous quantities (volumes, intensities) are skewed.

A **generalised** linear model keeps the linear predictor $\eta = X\beta$ but
connects it to the response's *mean* $\mu = \mathbb{E}[y]$ through a **link
function** $g$:

$$
g(\mu) = \eta = X\beta .
$$ {#eq-link}

The link maps the constrained mean onto the whole real line so a linear
predictor can range freely. For binary data the **logit** link
$g(\mu)=\log\frac{\mu}{1-\mu}$ turns a probability in $(0,1)$ into a log-odds in
$(-\infty,\infty)$; for counts the **log** link does the same for a positive
rate. Alongside the link, each response type has a **variance function**
$V(\mu)$ saying how the noise scales with the mean (constant for Gaussian,
$\mu(1-\mu)$ for binary, $\mu$ for Poisson counts).

These two objects — the link and the variance function — are exactly the two
fields that matter to fitting in a `nitrix` `Family` (the frozen record described
in [foundations §4](foundations.qmd#sec-expfam)). And they are not independent
free choices: the variance function is *forced* by the distribution through the
mean–variance identity $\operatorname{Var}[y]=\phi\,V(\mu)$ derived in
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-expfam). That identity is what makes the next
two sections inevitable rather than arbitrary.

In `nitrix` a response type is a small frozen `Family` value bundling the link
(`link` / `linkinv`), the slope $\mathrm{d}\mu/\mathrm{d}\eta$ (`mu_eta`), the
variance function (`variance`), a deviance (`unit_deviance`), and a log-likelihood
(`loglik`). `GAUSSIAN`, `BINOMIAL`, `POISSON`, `GAMMA`, `NEGBINOMIAL` and
`TWEEDIE` ship in the registry; `Family.with_link(...)` swaps in a non-canonical
link (`'probit'`, `'cloglog'`, `'sqrt'`, `'inverse'`); a fully custom one is
another `Family(...)` with your own fields.

## Where the working weights and response come from {#sec-working}

Before fitting, let us see that the weights $W$ and the working response $z$ are
*forced*, not chosen — the brief's first promise. Everything below flows from one
fact carried over from [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-expfam): for an
exponential family the mean determines the variance,

$$
\operatorname{Var}[y_i] = \phi\,V(\mu_i),\qquad \mu_i = g^{-1}(\eta_i),\qquad \eta_i = x_i\beta .
$$

**The score.** Maximising the likelihood means setting its gradient (the
**score**) to zero. For one observation the log-likelihood depends on $\beta$
only through $\mu_i$, which depends on $\beta$ only through $\eta_i = x_i\beta$.
The chain rule therefore threads through three links —
$\partial_\beta = \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu}\,\frac{\mathrm{d}\mu}{\mathrm{d}\eta}\,\frac{\partial\eta}{\partial\beta}$
— and a standard exponential-family fact (shown in
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-expfam)) is that
$\partial \ell/\partial\mu = (y-\mu)/(\phi V(\mu))$. Stacking over observations,
the score is

$$
U(\beta) \;=\; \frac{1}{\phi}\sum_{i} x_i^{\top}\,
\frac{(y_i-\mu_i)}{V(\mu_i)}\,\frac{\mathrm{d}\mu_i}{\mathrm{d}\eta_i}
\;=\; \frac{1}{\phi}\,X^{\top}\!\big(\text{residual, reweighted}\big).
$$ {#eq-score}

Read it: the score is the design matrix times the residual $y-\mu$, each
observation reweighted by how *sharply the mean responds to the predictor*
($\mathrm{d}\mu/\mathrm{d}\eta$) and how *noisy* it is ($1/V(\mu)$). An
observation in a flat region of the link, or in a high-variance region, gets a
quiet vote. Nothing here was invented; every factor is a derivative we were
forced to take.

**The Fisher information.** Newton's method needs the curvature — minus the
second derivative of the log-likelihood. Using the *expected* curvature instead
of the exact one (that choice is what distinguishes **Fisher scoring** from raw
Newton) makes the algebra collapse: the cross terms vanish in expectation because
$\mathbb{E}[y_i-\mu_i]=0$, and what survives is

$$
\mathcal{I}(\beta) \;=\; \frac{1}{\phi}\,X^{\top} W X,
\qquad
W = \operatorname{diag}(w_i),\quad
w_i = \frac{1}{V(\mu_i)}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}\mu_i}{\mathrm{d}\eta_i}\right)^{2} .
$$ {#eq-fisher}

There is your weight matrix, derived rather than declared: $W$ is the *only*
thing the curvature depends on, and each $w_i$ is the same two factors as the
score, now squared. This is exactly the `wts = dmu * dmu / variance` line in
`_irls._working` and the `w = mu_eta^2 / V` documented on `Family.with_link`.

**Assembling the update.** A Fisher-scoring step is
$\beta^{+} = \beta + \mathcal{I}^{-1}U$. Substitute @eq-score and @eq-fisher (the
$1/\phi$ cancels):

$$
X^{\top}WX\,\beta^{+} \;=\; X^{\top}WX\,\beta + X^{\top}WX\,\mathcal{I}^{-1}U
\;=\; X^{\top}W\Big(\underbrace{X\beta + (y-\mu)\,\tfrac{\mathrm{d}\eta}{\mathrm{d}\mu}}_{\textstyle z}\Big),
$$

where the last equality is a one-line rearrangement: $W^{-1}$ times the
reweighting in $U$ leaves exactly the residual on the $\eta$-scale,
$(y-\mu)\,\mathrm{d}\eta/\mathrm{d}\mu$, added to the current $\eta=X\beta$. Name
that bracket the **working response**

$$
\underbrace{z = \eta + (y - \mu)\,\frac{\mathrm{d}\eta}{\mathrm{d}\mu}}_{\text{working response}},
\qquad
\underbrace{W = \frac{1}{V(\mu)}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}\mu}{\mathrm{d}\eta}\right)^{2}}_{\text{working weights}} ,
$$ {#eq-working}

and the Fisher-scoring step *is* the weighted normal equations

$$
X^{\top} W X\,\beta^{+} = X^{\top} W z .
$$ {#eq-wls}

So $z$ is a first-order Taylor "linearised observation" — it maps each residual
back onto the linear-predictor scale — and $W$ downweights observations whose
mean sits in a high-variance or flat-link region. Both fell out of the score
equations; we did not get to pick them.

A subtlety `nitrix` handles in one place (`_irls.safe_dmu`): the factor
$\mathrm{d}\eta/\mathrm{d}\mu = 1/(\mathrm{d}\mu/\mathrm{d}\eta)$ in $z$ can be
*negative* — the inverse link is decreasing for the reciprocal/`'inverse'` link,
where $\mathrm{d}\mu/\mathrm{d}\eta = -1/\eta^2 < 0$. Its magnitude is therefore
floored away from zero **with its sign preserved**; a naive `clip(dmu, eps, None)`
would flip a derivative and make $z$ explode. For the increasing canonical links
this reduces to `max(dmu, eps)`.

@fig-glm-meanvar makes $W$ concrete: it plots $w_i$ across the range of $\mu$ for
three families, so you can *see* which observations IRLS listens to.

```{python}
#| label: fig-glm-meanvar
#| fig-cap: "Working weights $W=(\\mathrm{d}\\mu/\\mathrm{d}\\eta)^2/V(\\mu)$ as a function of the mean $\\mu$. Bernoulli weights peak at $\\mu=0.5$ and vanish at the certain extremes; Poisson weights grow with the rate (bigger counts are more informative); Gamma's log link exactly cancels its $V=\\mu^2$, leaving a flat weight."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Working weights W = (dmu/deta)^2 / V(mu) for three families, as a function of mu.
# Where W is large, the observation is "informative" about beta; where W -> 0,
# IRLS effectively ignores it.

mu_b = np.linspace(1e-3, 1 - 1e-3, 400)        # Bernoulli mean in (0,1)
# logit link: dmu/deta = mu(1-mu), V(mu) = mu(1-mu)  ->  W = mu(1-mu)
W_bern = mu_b * (1 - mu_b)

mu_p = np.linspace(1e-2, 8.0, 400)             # Poisson mean (positive rate)
# log link: dmu/deta = mu, V(mu) = mu          ->  W = mu
W_pois = mu_p

mu_g = np.linspace(1e-2, 8.0, 400)             # Gamma mean (positive)
# log link: dmu/deta = mu, V(mu) = mu^2        ->  W = 1 (constant)
W_gam = np.ones_like(mu_g)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10.5, 3.2))

axes[0].plot(mu_b, W_bern, lw=2, color="C0")
axes[0].axvline(0.5, ls=":", color="0.5")
axes[0].set(xlabel=r"$\mu$ (probability)", ylabel=r"working weight $W$",
            title="Bernoulli (logit)")
axes[0].annotate("peaks at $\\mu=0.5$", (0.5, 0.25), fontsize=8, ha="center")
axes[0].annotate("vanishes\nat extremes", (0.04, 0.02), fontsize=8)

axes[1].plot(mu_p, W_pois, lw=2, color="C1")
axes[1].set(xlabel=r"$\mu$ (rate)", title="Poisson (log)")
axes[1].annotate("grows with the mean:\nbig counts carry more", (0.4, 6.0),
                 fontsize=8)

axes[2].plot(mu_g, W_gam, lw=2, color="C2")
axes[2].set(xlabel=r"$\mu$", title=r"Gamma (log), $V=\mu^2$", ylim=(0, 2))
axes[2].annotate("flat: log link exactly\nbalances $V=\\mu^2$", (0.4, 1.3),
                 fontsize=8)

fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## IRLS, rigorously: it *is* Fisher scoring {#sec-irls}

Section 4 did the heavy lifting; here is the loop it justifies, stated cleanly.
Given a current $\beta$ with $\eta=X\beta$ and $\mu=g^{-1}(\eta)$:

1. compute the working weights $W$ and working response $z$ of @eq-working;
2. solve the weighted normal equations @eq-wls for a new $\beta$;
3. recompute $\eta,\mu,z,W$ and repeat until $\beta$ stops moving.

This is **iteratively reweighted least squares** (IRLS): the same $p\times p$
solve as ordinary regression, wrapped in a loop that refreshes the weights and
the working response. Ordinary least squares is the special case $W=I$, $z=y$ —
the link is the identity and $V$ is constant, so the working response *is* the
data and the loop converges in a single step (which is why `glm_fit` short-circuits
the Gaussian case to `_ols_fit`).

The point worth dwelling on: because @eq-wls was *derived* as a Fisher-scoring
step (not posited), IRLS inherits Newton's quadratic local convergence. Near the
optimum the working linearisation is excellent and the error squares each
iteration — which is exactly the few-step plateau you will see in @fig-irls.

This single penalised-IRLS solve is the literal core shared by GLM and GAM. In
the source it is `fit_penalised_irls` in `nitrix.stats._irls`, and its inner
`step` is one line — `a_inv @ (Xw.T @ z)` with `a = Xw.T @ X + penalty + ridge`.
GLM calls it with the penalty matrix $S = 0$ (`glm.py` passes
`penalty = jnp.zeros((p, p))`); the GAM rung calls the *same* function with a
non-zero $S$. The warm start (`irls_warm_start`) is itself one weighted least-
squares fit of the linked initial mean $g(\text{init\_mu}(y))$, so the loop begins
near the answer.

For the *robustness* of that loop — what to do when a raw Newton/Fisher step
overshoots and the likelihood goes down — we hand off to the shared machinery
page rather than re-derive it here. The fix is **damping**: take the Newton
direction but a safe fraction of its length, equivalently add a small ridge
$\lambda I$ to the curvature $X^{\top}WX$ before solving (large $\lambda$ → a
short, gradient-like step; small $\lambda$ → the full Newton step near the
optimum). The full story — line search, the Levenberg ridge, why the small dense
solve stays SPD — lives in [solvers §3](solvers.qmd#sec-newton), and the small
$p\times p$ solve itself in [solvers §2](solvers.qmd#sec-cholesky). `nitrix` runs
a **fixed** budget of IRLS iterations (`n_iter`, default 25) so the whole fit is
`vmap`-clean and JIT-compiled across the image, and it evaluates the final
covariance $V=(X^{\top}WX + S + \text{ridge})^{-1}$ *at the converged* $\beta$ —
the correct point for standard errors (the comment in `_irls.py` flags this
final re-evaluation as intentional, not redundant).

## The deviance: the family's natural loss

We keep saying "until $\beta$ stops moving" and "goodness of fit" — but on what
scale? For Gaussian data the obvious yardstick is the residual sum of squares.
The **deviance** is the right generalisation, and it is built from one idea:
compare your model to the best a model of this family *could possibly* do.

Imagine a model so flexible it has one free mean per observation — it can set
$\hat\mu_i = y_i$ exactly. This is the **saturated model**; it fits the data
perfectly and is the ceiling on log-likelihood. The deviance measures how far
*below* that ceiling your model sits, on twice the log-likelihood scale:

$$
D \;=\; 2\big(\ell_{\text{saturated}} - \ell_{\text{model}}\big)
\;=\; \sum_i d(y_i,\hat\mu_i),
$$ {#eq-deviance}

where the per-observation contribution $d(y_i,\mu_i)$ is the family's
`unit_deviance`. Two features earn the definition its keep. First, it is always
$\ge 0$ and equals $0$ only for a perfect fit — a genuine loss. Second, for the
Gaussian family $d(y,\mu)=(y-\mu)^2$ (read it straight off `GAUSSIAN.unit_deviance`
in `_family.py`), so the deviance is *exactly* the residual sum of squares and
this whole framework reduces to least squares. For binary data $d$ is the
familiar $2\big[y\log\frac{y}{\mu}+(1-y)\log\frac{1-y}{1-\mu}\big]$; for Poisson,
$2\big[y\log\frac{y}{\mu}-(y-\mu)\big]$ — both visible in `_binomial_deviance` /
`_poisson_deviance`.

`nitrix` uses the deviance for three things. (1) **Goodness of fit**:
`deviance_explained` returns $1 - D/D_0$ where $D_0$ is the
`null_deviance` (the intercept-only fit), the family-general cousin of $R^2$ —
and for Gaussian it *is* $R^2$, since then $D$ is the residual and $D_0$ the
total sum of squares. (2) The **dispersion** estimate $\hat\phi = D/(N-p)$ for
the families that estimate it (Gaussian, Gamma; the fixed-dispersion families
report $\phi=1$). (3) Implicitly, **convergence assurance**: because IRLS is
Fisher scoring, the deviance drops fast and plateaus within a handful of steps
(@fig-irls), which is what makes a *fixed* iteration budget safe — there is no
adaptive stopping test to break the `vmap`, and the budget is comfortably past
the plateau.

## A worked IRLS, by hand

To make IRLS concrete, here is a *logistic* regression fit by the loop above —
pure NumPy, no `nitrix`, runs at doc-build time. Watch the weighted least-squares
solve repeat a handful of times and stop.

```{python}
#| label: fig-irls
#| fig-cap: "IRLS fitting a logistic regression. Left: the log-likelihood climbs and plateaus in ~5 reweighted least-squares solves. Right: the recovered probability curve."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
N = 400
x = np.linspace(-3, 3, N)
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(N), x])           # intercept + slope
beta_true = np.array([-0.4, 1.6])
p_true = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-X @ beta_true))
y = (rng.uniform(size=N) < p_true).astype(float)

def loglik(beta):
    eta = X @ beta
    return np.sum(y * eta - np.log1p(np.exp(eta)))

beta = np.zeros(2)                              # start at the origin
hist = [loglik(beta)]
for _ in range(8):
    eta = X @ beta
    mu = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-eta))                 # inverse logit link
    W = np.maximum(mu * (1 - mu), 1e-6)         # working weights  V(mu) (dmu/deta)^2
    z = eta + (y - mu) / W                      # working response
    # weighted normal equations  (XᵀWX) beta = XᵀWz   — the same p×p solve
    XtW = X.T * W
    beta = np.linalg.solve(XtW @ X, XtW @ z)
    hist.append(loglik(beta))

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.3))
axL.plot(hist, marker="o", ms=4)
axL.set(xlabel="IRLS iteration", ylabel="log-likelihood", title="convergence")
order = np.argsort(x)
axR.scatter(x, y, s=8, alpha=0.25, label="data")
axR.plot(x[order], (1/(1+np.exp(-X@beta)))[order], lw=2, label="fitted P(y=1)")
axR.plot(x[order], p_true[order], lw=1, ls="--", label="truth")
axR.legend(fontsize=8); axR.set_title("logistic fit")
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print(f"converged β ≈ {beta.round(3)}   (true {beta_true})")
```

For the logistic family $W=\mu(1-\mu)$ and the bracketed update is exactly
@eq-wls — the same `z`/`W` you derived in §4, now in five lines of NumPy. The
log-likelihood rises and flattens within a few steps: this is the fast Fisher-
scoring convergence that lets `glm_fit` run a fixed iteration budget over a whole
brain.

## The solve itself: small, and cuSOLVER-free

Every step is one $p\times p$ solve, and $p$ is tiny — so the solver can be too.
`nitrix` routes all of them through a hand-written `small_inv_logdet`
(`linalg/_smalllinalg.py`): a closed-form inverse for $p\le 2$, and a hand-coded
Cholesky factorisation plus triangular solves for larger $p$, returning both the
inverse and the log-determinant in one pass. Crucially it makes **no `cuSOLVER`
custom call**. On the GPU stacks where the vendor solver is broken, the textbook
`jnp.linalg.solve` would fail; this hand-rolled path keeps the entire suite
running on the accelerator. The full derivation of that routine — and why it is
numerically sound — is [solvers §2](solvers.qmd#sec-cholesky); the point to carry
forward is that GLM, GAM, contrasts and the family zoo all funnel through this one
solve.

## From a fit to an answer: contrasts and p-values {#sec-contrasts}

A fitted $\beta$ is rarely the end; you want to test a hypothesis — "is this
coefficient nonzero?", "does this group differ?". The grammar for asking is a
**contrast**.

**A contrast is a question phrased as a vector.** Pick a vector $c\in\mathbb{R}^p$
and the linear combination $c^{\top}\beta$ *is* your question: $c=(0,1,0,\dots)$
asks about the second coefficient; $c=(0,0,1,-1,\dots)$ asks whether two group
means differ. The estimate of the answer is the same linear combination of the fit, $c^{\top}\hat\beta$, and because
$\hat\beta$ has covariance $V$, the estimate's variance is the quadratic form

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\big(c^{\top}\hat\beta\big) = c^{\top} V c,
\qquad V = \hat\phi\,(X^{\top}WX)^{-1} .
$$ {#eq-contrast-var}

Read $V$ straight off the inverse Fisher information from §4: the curvature *is*
the precision of $\hat\beta$, so its inverse is the covariance, scaled by the
dispersion $\hat\phi$. In `glm.py` the unscaled $(X^{\top}WX)^{-1}$ is stored as
`cov_unscaled` and `t_contrast` forms `dispersion * einsum('...ij,i,j->...',
cov_unscaled, c, c)` — literally @eq-contrast-var.

**The t-statistic.** Standardise the estimate by its own standard error:

$$
t = \frac{c^{\top}\hat\beta}{\sqrt{c^{\top}Vc}} .
$$

Under the null $c^{\top}\beta=0$, this follows a Student-$t$ distribution on
$N-p$ residual degrees of freedom (`result.dof_resid`). `t_contrast` returns the
4-tuple `(effect, se, t, p_value)` — the contrast estimate, its standard error,
the statistic, and the two-sided p-value — which are the ModelArray `lm` output
columns.

**The p-value, without a vendor solver.** A p-value is a tail probability of that
$t$ distribution, and here is the load-bearing identity the source uses: the
Student-$t$ tail is a value of the **regularised incomplete beta function**
$I_x(a,b)$. Concretely the two-sided survival probability is

$$
P\big(|T| > |t|\big) = I_{x}\!\left(\tfrac{\nu}{2}, \tfrac12\right),
\qquad x = \frac{\nu}{\nu + t^2},\quad \nu = N-p,
$$ {#eq-tbeta}

which is exactly `_t_two_sided_sf` in `glm.py`:
`betainc(0.5*df, 0.5, df/(df + t*t))`. Why phrase it through $I_x$ at all? Because
$I_x(a,b)$ has a portable, differentiable implementation
(`jax.scipy.special.betainc`) that needs **no** vendor special-function or linear-
algebra call — so the *inference* is as portable and as accelerator-clean as the
*fit*. (The same identity, with different shape parameters, gives the $F$ tail in
`_f_sf` and the t-quantile inverted by a few Newton steps in `_effect._t_ppf`,
which backs `confidence_interval`.)

**Multi-row contrasts: the $F$-test.** Sometimes the question has several parts at
once — "does *any* of these three coefficients differ from zero?". Stack the
questions as the rows of a matrix $C$ ($m\times p$) and test $C\beta=0$ jointly.
The natural statistic is the squared length of $C\hat\beta$ in the metric of its
own covariance (a Wald quadratic form), divided by its rank:

$$
F = \frac{1}{m}\,(C\hat\beta)^{\top}\,(C V C^{\top})^{-1}\,(C\hat\beta) ,
$$ {#eq-fstat}

which `f_contrast` computes — `mat = C @ cov @ C.T`, inverted by the same
`small_inv_logdet`, then `quad / (m * dispersion)` — returning `(F, p_value, df1,
df2)` on $(m,\,N-p)$ degrees of freedom with the p-value from @eq-tbeta's
$F$-cousin. For *nested* model comparison `compare_models` offers the
extra-sum-of-squares $F$-test (Gaussian) or the likelihood-ratio test
$2(\ell_{\text{full}}-\ell_{\text{reduced}})\sim\chi^2$ (other families).

**The result object.** All of this hangs off a single frozen-dataclass
`GLMResult` (`glm.py`), registered as a JAX pytree by `register_result`
(`_result.py`) so it stacks cleanly under `vmap` and survives `jit` — the array
fields (`coef`, `cov_unscaled`, `dispersion`, `deviance`, `null_deviance`,
`log_lik`) ride as traced children, the metadata (`family`, `n_obs`, `rank`) as
static aux. The fitter-agnostic readouts in `_effect.py` (`confidence_interval`,
`standardized_effect`) then turn an `(effect, se, dof)` triple into the
confidence interval or Cohen's-d-style ratio a report actually quotes — also
cuSOLVER-free, the t-quantile found by Newton iteration on the same `betainc` CDF.

## The family zoo, grounded {#sec-zoo}

The IRLS core serves every classic GLM family by swapping the `Family` record;
`_family.py` ships `GAUSSIAN`, `BINOMIAL`, `POISSON`, `GAMMA`, `NEGBINOMIAL`
(overdispersed counts, $V(\mu)=\mu+\alpha\mu^2$) and `TWEEDIE` (the semicontinuous
$1<p<2$ regime), with `Family.with_link` composing probit / cloglog / sqrt /
inverse links. But `nitrix.stats` is deliberately broader than "logistic plus a
few links": some responses break the *one linear predictor, fixed dispersion*
assumption of the scalar IRLS core and get their own fitter. Three worth knowing,
each grounded in real source:

- **Beta regression** (`betareg.py`, `beta_fit`) for proportions in $(0,1)$ —
  tissue fractions, fractional occupancy. The model is $y\sim\text{Beta}(\mu\phi,
  (1-\mu)\phi)$ with $\mu=\text{logit}^{-1}(X\beta)$ and an estimated **precision**
  $\phi$. Its score is *digamma*-based, not the $\mathrm{d}\mu/\mathrm{d}\eta^2/V$
  weight, so it alternates an IRLS-style step for $\beta$ with a Newton step for
  $\log\phi$ (Ferrari & Cribari-Neto 2004); the returned `cov_unscaled` is the
  $\beta$ block of the *joint* $(\beta,\phi)$ information inverse, so the standard
  errors honestly account for estimating $\phi$.

- **Ordinal / cumulative-link** (`ordinal.py`, `ordinal_fit`) for ordered
  categories — clinical stage, a Likert rating. It places $K-1$ ordered thresholds
  and a *single* shared slope (the proportional-odds assumption),
  $P(y\le k)=F(\theta_k - x^{\top}\beta)$ with $F$ the logistic or normal CDF. The
  thresholds are kept ordered by the unconstrained reparameterisation
  $\theta=\theta_1+\text{cumsum}([0,e^{\delta}])$ and the concave log-likelihood is
  maximised by the shared damped Newton — the same [solvers
  §3](solvers.qmd#sec-newton) routine.

- **Gaussian location-scale** (`gaulss.py`, `gaulss_fit`) — the bridge toward
  GAMLSS. The ordinary GLM gives the *mean* a linear predictor and treats the
  scale as one nuisance number; `gaulss` gives the **scale its own** linear
  predictor, $y_i\sim N(\mu_i,\sigma_i^2)$ with $\mu_i=x_i^{\top}\beta_\mu$ and
  $\log\sigma_i = z_i^{\top}\beta_\sigma$, modelling heteroscedasticity directly.
  The Gaussian location-scale expected information is *block-diagonal* between the
  two coefficient blocks (because $\mathbb{E}[y-\mu]=0$ kills the cross term), so
  each Fisher-scoring step is two decoupled `small_inv_logdet` solves — a clean
  illustration that "model the variance too" costs only a second weighted solve.

Every one of these is `vmap`-clean over voxels and cuSOLVER-free, fit by the same
Fisher-scoring-plus-small-solve pattern this page is built on.

## Where this goes

Everything above recurs:

- Add a **penalty matrix** $S$ to @eq-wls, $(X^{\top}W X + S)\beta = X^{\top}W z$,
  and the smooth, wiggly terms of a **GAM** drop out — with $S$'s strength chosen
  by the [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd#sec-fs-update) update.
- Add **random effects** and the noise becomes correlated; estimating its
  structure is the [linear mixed model](lme.qmd#sec-reml), where the same Newton
  loop runs on a cleverly **diagonalised** covariance.
- Combine the two and you have **GLMMs** and **GAMMs**.

The working response, the working weights, the small cuSOLVER-free solve, and
damped Newton are the constants; each later chapter changes only what sits inside
or beside the $p\times p$ system.

## Further reading

- Nelder, J. A. & Wedderburn, R. W. M. (1972). *Generalized Linear Models.*
  Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series A, 135(3), 370–384 — the
  original paper that unified the families and introduced IRLS.
- McCullagh, P. & Nelder, J. A. (1989). *Generalized Linear Models*, 2nd ed.
  Chapman & Hall/CRC — the standard reference; deviance, links, variance functions.
- Green, P. J. (1984). *Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares for Maximum
  Likelihood Estimation, and some Robust and Resistant Alternatives.* Journal of
  the Royal Statistical Society, Series B, 46(2), 149–192 — IRLS as Fisher
  scoring and its role in penalised likelihood.
- Agresti, A. (2015). *Foundations of Linear and Generalized Linear Models.*
  Wiley — a modern, readable development from the linear model upward.
- Ferrari, S. & Cribari-Neto, F. (2004). *Beta Regression for Modelling Rates and
  Proportions.* Journal of Applied Statistics, 31(7), 799–815 — the estimator
  behind `beta_fit`.
- Rigby, R. A. & Stasinopoulos, D. M. (2005). *Generalized Additive Models for
  Location, Scale and Shape.* Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series C,
  54(3), 507–554 — the GAMLSS framework that `gaulss` previews.

## See also

- Code: [`nitrix/stats/glm.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/glm.py),
  the shared solver [`stats/_irls.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/_irls.py),
  the family records [`stats/_family.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/_family.py),
  the result/effect helpers [`stats/_result.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/_result.py)
  and [`stats/_effect.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/_effect.py),
  the family zoo [`stats/betareg.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/betareg.py),
  [`stats/ordinal.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/ordinal.py),
  [`stats/gaulss.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/gaulss.py),
  and the small dense solve
  [`linalg/_smalllinalg.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_smalllinalg.py).
- Builds on: [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-expfam) (the exponential family and
  mean–variance identity) and [solvers](solvers.qmd#sec-newton) (the small dense
  solve and damped Newton).
- Next rung: [linear mixed-effects models](lme.qmd#sec-reml) (random effects and
  variance components); and [smoothing-parameter
  selection](fellner-schall.qmd#sec-fs-update) for the penalty $S$ that turns a
  GLM into a GAM.
